title: Imre Simon Test-of-Time Award 2026
content:
The awarded article was published in LATIN 2006 under title "Position-Restricted Substring Search" and extended later into Theoretical Computer Science "Rank and Select Revisited and Extended". 

The main contribution of the article was to draw connections between results discovered in different subfields. For example, fractional cascading to speed-up geometric data structures was shown to be efficiently implementable with wavelet trees developed for string algorithms. Later research has benefited from these insights to e.g. cast known lower-bounds from the geometric setting to string algorithms.

title: Ongoing SAFER projects
content:
POLYDEC project (2024-2026) focuses on nuclear decontamination with polyelectrolyte gels. The main application is in nuclear power plants and other facilities, but the product has use in cleanup of polluted environment as well. 

MEDERA project (2026-2028) deals with radioanalytical method development for difficult-to-measure beta emitters in challenging sample matrices. We aim to establish new radioanalytical services with a competitive pricing.

title: Three HCAS Fellows awarded major research funding
content:
Helsinki Collegium for Advanced Studies Core Fellow Olesya Khanina, recently appointed Professor of Finno-Ugric Linguistics at the University of Helsinki, has received an ERC Advanced Grant for her project “Uneven Language Change” (UCHANGE). The five-year project (2027–2031) investigates how different levels of language – lexicon, phonology, morphology, and syntax – change at different rates over time, and how these differences can be used to reconstruct linguistic and social histories.

Using the Uralic language family as a case study, the project develops innovative methods for collecting and annotating large-scale linguistic data across multiple language levels and for modelling long-term language evolution.

“UCHANGE comes at a moment when language data are expanding rapidly, including data for indigenous languages all across the world. However, the new ‘big data’ of historical linguistics can produce meaningful results only if they are compiled and processed by experts in these languages; otherwise no computational methods can save us from the ‘garbage in – garbage out’ effect,” Khanina explains.
snippet: Helsinki Collegium for Advanced Studies Core Fellow Olesya Khanina, recently appointed Professor of Finno-Ugric Linguistics at the University of Helsinki, has received an ERC Advanced Grant for her
creator: visiiri@helsinki.fi

title: Helsingin yliopisto lisää avoimen väylän aloituspaikkoja
content:
The University of Helsinki is increasing the number of student places offered through the open university route in its bachelor’s programmes. The goal is that, in the coming years, 10% of places in these programmes will be allocated to applicants who have previously completed open university studies. In many disciplines, this is already the case. 

“Experience has shown that students admitted through the open university route are motivated and committed to their studies. They already have a realistic understanding of university studies, and many have completed parts of their degrees before becoming degree students,” says Head of Services Jenni Krapu. 

Developing the open university route is important to the University of Helsinki, as it enables students to demonstrate their skills on the basis of completed studies. This is opposed to admissions being based solely on entrance examinations, the Finnish matriculation examination, or equivalent international qualifications. In addition, increasing intake through the route makes admissions routes overall increasingly equal.

title: Do bears and beekeepers get along? Insights from a Beekeeping Conference
content:
The bear population of Finland has increased rapidly over the last few years. More and more beekeepers have to prepare for the risk of a bear visiting their apiaries, i.e. sets of beehives. The state-sponsored way of preparation is to surround your hives with an electric fence. However, the effectiveness of the fences in preventing bear damages and the workload added by their maintenance to the beekeepers’ work routine remain unknown. 

Kasper is currently investigating how often bears visit fenced apiaries and how often the fences curb bear efforts to satisfy their hunger for honey. He also aims to investigate whether sites in different environments face a higher risk of bear damages, and if certain types of bears are more likely to visit apiaries. Kasper shared his preliminary finding that the fences seem to be an effective way to reduce bear damages, but they require further development to plug key gaps in function.
snippet: The bear population of Finland has increased rapidly over the last few years. More and more beekeepers have to prepare for the risk of a bear visiting their apiaries, i.e. sets of beehives. The
creator: lucasb@helsinki.fi

title: Emissions from plankton help in forming cloud seeds over the world's oceans
content:
For nearly fifty years, scientists have suspected that microscopic marine plankton play a role in cloud formation over the oceans. Now, an experiment led by the University of Helsinki has suggested that it may be more important than previously thought.

Marine plankton release dimethylsulfide (DMS) during photosynthesis — the gas responsible for the characteristic smell of the sea. When oxidised in the atmosphere, DMS forms acidic vapors.

One of these is methanesulfonic acid (MSA). has shown that this previously overlooked gas can trigger the formation and growth of aerosol particles over cold, pristine ocean regions. Moisture can condense around these aerosol particles, eventually forming clouds. Since this mechanism is not accounted for in current climate models, the experimental results not only deepen our understanding of the atmosphere, but also improve the accuracy of climate models.

title: Arvostettu EU:n miljoonarahoitus viidelle Helsingin yliopiston tutkijalle
content:
Five researchers working at the University of Helsinki have been awarded esteemed Advanced Grants by the European Research Council. This €2.5-million grant scheme is targeted at leading principal investigators who wish to pursue groundbreaking projects with the potential for extremely high-level impact. 

Professor ’s (Faculty of Humanities) project develops new methods for tracing prehistoric migration and cultural contacts by analysing language change. Comparing changes at different levels of language, such as lexicon, phonology, morphology and syntax, makes it possible to reconstruct a more accurate map of the movement and interaction of ancient communities.

Khanina’s group investigates Uralic languages, which comprise seven to nine branches and roughly 50 modern languages, all of which originate in the same ancestor language. The researchers will examine how languages develop and how societal conditions affect this development. 

The approach to be developed in the project can be applied to any sufficiently documented language family. The goal is to expand the opportunities of linguistics in the study of pre- and early history.

creator: katitoiv@helsinki.fi

content:
In this seminar, university lecturer Sonja Laine and university instructor Sakari Hyrkkö present their research with a theme "On educational interventions: two case examples and discussion" followed by a joint discussion.

title: Puutarhaopastukset 2026
content:
On a guided tour, you’ll journey through millions of years of evolution along the paths of the Evolution Garden, and immerse yourself in a world of scents and colours in the Garden of the senses. You’ll also admire unique lichen and moss gardens, while hearing stories about the garden’s past and present, and the buildings found in the area.

Tours will take place from June 23 to August 22, 2026. Free entrance, no prior registration required.

  • Tuesdays, Fridays and Saturdays at 15 (3 pm)

    • The tour starts at the greenhouse entrance and lasts approximately 40 minutes.

    Kaisaniemenranta 2, Helsinki

title: Arkadiankadun siltaremontti aiheuttaa muutoksia liikennejärjestelyihin Luonnontieteellisen museon lähistöllä
content:
The renovation will start with the construction of temporary traffic arrangements from Wednesday 7 January onwards. The renovation is expected to be completed in late 2026.

A temporary bridge for pedestrians will be built on the east side of Arkadiankatu over the Baana. The aim is to maintain the pedestrian route on the east side of the bridge until the temporary bridge is operational. Cyclists will also be able to use the bridge.

The Baana route underneath the bridge is expected to remain open for most of the renovation's duration, with some minor exceptions.

There will be changes to the motor vehicle routes by the bridge and around it in January. If possible, should be favoured during the bridge renovation.

Connections to the bus terminals in Kamppi will remain available, but there will be a slight diversion of routes.

Trams 1 and 1T will run via Simonkatu and Kamppi. Lasipalatsi and the Natural History Museum will not be served.

The diversion is due to the renovation of the Arkadiankatu bridge and will remain in place until late 2026. The renovation will not affect HSL's bus services. 

Pohjoinen Rautatiekatu 13

title: Ukrainan sota - yliopiston suositukset
content:
The University of Helsinki has condemned the military action by Russia and expressed its support for Ukraine and its university community. We have also expressed our support for Ukraine, together with other Finnish universities, through statements by and the . As a demonstration of support, we have illuminated buildings in the colours of the Ukrainian flag and raised the flag on our premises.

One of the principles of the University of Helsinki is to promote international collaboration in research and higher education, and to support these efforts even in difficult circumstances. Russia’s indefensible military aggression in Ukraine has required exceptional measures, which include those taken in the collaboration between universities.

The University of Helsinki is monitoring both national and EU-level sanctions, adapting our policies and processes accordingly. Together with other Finnish and international universities, the University of Helsinki strives to promote the end of military action in Ukraine, while supporting Ukrainian institutions and people.
snippet: The University of Helsinki has condemned the military action by Russia and expressed its support for Ukraine and its university community. We have also expressed our support for Ukraine, together with
creator: terojuut@helsinki.fi

title: Doctoral researcher Roya presented the development of biofilm models at Microbiology Days 2026
content:
Roya's work is part of the project "Innovative therapeutics and drug discovery tools to battle against antimicrobial resistant biofilms in urinary tract infections", which aims to develop novel therapeutics and screening tools to combat antibiotic-resistant biofilms associated with urinary tract infections.

Roya shared recent findings from her doctoral research on the development of a catheter-based model to study biofilms formed by uropathogenic Escherichia coli (UPEC). The model is designed to better mimic clinically relevant conditions and support the discovery of new strategies to prevent and treat biofilm-associated urinary tract infections.

The project is funded by the Research Council of Finland through a Proof-of-Concept Grant (No. 365302, 2025–2026).
